united_kingdom_of_great_britain_and_irel.name: British Empire
united_kingdom_of_great_britain_and_irel.summary:
In 1914, the British Empire is at the absolute apex of its power — controlling roughly 25% of the Earth's land surface and governing perhaps 400 million people. The Royal Navy rules every ocean. British industry, banking, and commerce dominate the world economy. London is the financial capital of the world, and the pound sterling is the global reserve currency.

Yet Britain is anxious. Germany's rapid industrialization and naval buildup directly challenge British supremacy. The assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand in Sarajevo on June 28, 1914 sets off a chain of treaty obligations that will pull every major power into the most devastating war in human history to that point — a war that will end the British Empire's unchallenged supremacy, though the empire itself will survive for another three decades.
united_kingdom_of_great_britain_and_irel.ruler: King George V (r. 1910–1936); Prime Minister H.H. Asquith
key_events: Britain governs 25% of the Earth's land — the largest empire in history
Royal Navy outbuilds Germany in the 'Dreadnought race'
Assassination of Franz Ferdinand (June 28) triggers World War I
Britain declares war on Germany (August 4, 1914)
united_kingdom_of_great_britain_and_irel.did_you_know: The British Empire was so vast that it coined the phrase 'the sun never sets on the British Empire' — because at any moment, some part of the empire was in daylight. Managing time zones across this empire was one factor that led to the establishment of Greenwich Mean Time as the global standard.

german_empire.name: German Empire
german_empire.summary:
Unified only in 1871 under Prussian leadership, Germany has in four decades become Europe's most powerful industrial and military nation. German steel production exceeds Britain's; German universities lead the world in science and engineering; the German army is the finest professional fighting force in Europe. But Germany feels encircled — by France to the west, Russia to the east, and Britain's naval supremacy cutting off German access to global trade.

Kaiser Wilhelm II's ambitions for German 'Weltpolitik' (world policy) have alarmed every major power. The assassination of Franz Ferdinand provides the spark. Germany's Schlieffen Plan calls for defeating France in six weeks, then turning east to face Russia — a plan that will fail catastrophically, leading to four years of unimaginable slaughter.
key_events: Germany's industrial output now rivals Britain — the dominant European power
Blank check to Austria-Hungary escalates the July Crisis
Schlieffen Plan — invade France through Belgium, triggering British entry
U-boat warfare will eventually draw the United States into the war
german_empire.did_you_know: Kaiser Wilhelm II was Queen Victoria's grandson — he was a first cousin of both King George V of Britain and Tsar Nicholas II of Russia. The First World War was in part a family quarrel at the dynastic level, even as millions of ordinary men died in the trenches.

russian_empire.name: Russian Empire
russian_empire.summary:
The Russian Empire in 1914 is enormous but dangerously fragile. Tsar Nicholas II rules over 170 million people across 11 time zones, but his autocratic system is creaking under the strain of modernity. The 1905 Revolution, triggered by the catastrophic defeat to Japan, forced a constitutional parliament (Duma) on the Tsar, but real power remains autocratic and unresponsive.

Russia mobilizes in defense of Serbia, its Slavic ally, triggering German war plans. The Russian army is vast but poorly equipped and supplied. Defeats at Tannenberg and the Masurian Lakes will be catastrophic. By 1917, military collapse, food shortages, and revolutionary pressure will end three centuries of Romanov rule.
key_events: Russia mobilizes for Serbia — triggering German declaration of war
Defeat at Tannenberg (1914) destroys an entire Russian army
Rasputin's influence over the royal family scandalizes Russia
Russian Revolution (1917) will end the Romanov dynasty
russian_empire.did_you_know: Russia's defeat to Japan in 1904–1905 was the first time an Asian nation had defeated a European great power in modern warfare. It shocked the world, triggered the 1905 Russian Revolution, and inspired independence movements across Asia and Africa.

ottoman_empire.name: Ottoman Empire
ottoman_empire.summary:
By 1914 the Ottoman Empire has lost most of its European territory in the Balkan Wars (1912–13) — the 'sick man of Europe' is genuinely dying. The Young Turk government (Committee of Union and Progress) has seized power promising modernization, but faces rebellion in Arabia, Armenian resistance in Anatolia, and financial dependence on German banks.

The Ottomans enter World War I on the German side in October 1914, hoping to recover lost territory and escape the chokehold of British and French creditors. Instead, British forces will advance from Egypt and India, Arab revolt will begin in 1916, and by 1918 the empire will be finished — partitioned into the modern states of Turkey, Syria, Iraq, Jordan, Israel/Palestine, and Saudi Arabia.
ottoman_empire.ruler: Sultan Mehmed V (figurehead); Enver Pasha, Talaat Pasha (real power)
key_events: Balkan Wars (1912–13) strip away most remaining European territory
Young Turk government takes power promising reform
Ottomans join WWI on German side (October 1914)
Armenian Genocide (1915–1916) — estimated 600,000–1.5 million killed
ottoman_empire.did_you_know: The Ottoman Empire's partition after WWI was largely decided in secret — the Sykes-Picot Agreement (1916) between Britain and France drew borders across the Middle East with a ruler, dividing territories based on European interests rather than local ethnic, religious, or tribal realities. The consequences of those arbitrarily drawn lines are still felt today.

united_states.name: United States
united_states.summary:
The United States in 1914 is the world's largest economy but not yet a major military power by European standards. Woodrow Wilson wins the presidency promising domestic reform and non-involvement in European affairs. When war breaks out in Europe in August 1914, the United States declares neutrality — a position that most Americans strongly support.

But America's industrial might makes neutrality increasingly difficult to maintain. Both sides need American goods, credit, and eventually manpower. Germany's unrestricted submarine warfare, sinking American ships, and the Zimmermann Telegram (proposing German-Mexican alliance against the U.S.) will push Wilson to ask Congress for war in April 1917.
key_events: US is the world's largest economy but officially neutral in WWI
Sinking of the Lusitania (1915) — 1,198 killed, including 128 Americans
Zimmermann Telegram (1917) — Germany proposes Mexico attack the US
US enters WWI (April 1917) — tipping the balance decisively against Germany
united_states.did_you_know: When American troops finally arrived in France in 1917–18, they brought something as valuable as fresh soldiers: an immune system naive to the European strains of disease that had decimated both sides. But they also brought the 1918 influenza pandemic, which would kill more people than the entire war.

austria_hungary.name: Austro-Hungarian Empire
austria_hungary.summary:
The Austro-Hungarian Empire — a 'multinational state' governing Germans, Hungarians, Czechs, Slovaks, Poles, Ukrainians, Romanians, Slovenes, Croats, and Serbs — is held together primarily by the elderly Emperor Franz Joseph I, who has reigned since 1848. When he dies in 1916, the empire has no obvious reason to continue existing.

It is Austro-Hungary's ultimatum to Serbia following the assassination of Archduke Franz Ferdinand that starts the war. The empire hoped for a small, punitive war to reassert its authority in the Balkans — instead it triggered the largest war in history to that point, one that will kill the empire itself by 1918.
key_events: Archduke Franz Ferdinand assassinated in Sarajevo (June 28, 1914)
Austria-Hungary's ultimatum to Serbia sparks WWI
Franz Joseph I — emperor for 68 years — a symbol of the old order
Empire disintegrates in 1918 into Austria, Hungary, Czechoslovakia, Yugoslavia
austria_hungary.did_you_know: Franz Joseph I reigned for 68 years — the third longest reign of any monarch in European history. He came to the throne in 1848 during revolutionary upheaval and died in 1916 during the most catastrophic war the world had ever seen, outlasting every institution he had known.

france.name: French Republic / Colonial Empire
france.summary:
France in 1914 is the world's second largest colonial empire after Britain, governing 60 million colonial subjects in North Africa, West Africa, Indochina, and the Pacific. Domestically, France is a vibrant if politically divided republic — the Third Republic has survived the trauma of the Prussian defeat of 1871 and the humiliation of Alsace-Lorraine's loss.

France's alliance with Russia (Franco-Russian Alliance, 1894) and informal entente with Britain (Entente Cordiale, 1904) position it against Germany and Austria-Hungary. The French army, rebuilt since 1871, goes to war in 1914 with offensive doctrine and insufficient respect for machine guns and artillery — early losses are catastrophic. But France will endure four years of trench warfare on its own soil.
france.population_estimate: ~40 million in France; ~60 million in colonies
france.ruler: President Raymond Poincaré; Prime Minister René Viviani
key_events: Alsace-Lorraine still lost to Germany since 1871 — revenge is a national obsession
Franco-Russian Alliance and Entente Cordiale align France against Germany
Battle of the Marne (1914) stops German advance 30 miles from Paris
Verdun (1916) — France and Germany each lose hundreds of thousands
france.did_you_know: The Battle of Verdun (February–December 1916) lasted 303 days and killed approximately 700,000 soldiers — roughly 2,300 per day. The fortifications at Verdun still contain the remains of an estimated 130,000 unidentified soldiers. Verdun is the longest battle in history.
